mnemonics.title: IoT Security Layers
mnemonics.content: Please Don't Throw Sausage Pizza Away - Perception, Data/Network, Transport, Security, Processing, Application, Analytics (using first letters)

mnemonics.title: IoT Attack Vectors
mnemonics.content: CUPS-FM: Credentials, Unencrypted, Physical, Software, Firmware, APIs, MitM - the main ways hackers get in

mnemonics.title: Secure IoT Design Principles
mnemonics.content: SLIDE: Segmentation, Least Privilege, Integrity, Defense in Depth, Encryption - key security pillars

acronyms.usage: Network of interconnected physical devices that collect and exchange data
Lightweight publish-subscribe messaging protocol for IoT
RESTful web transfer protocol for resource-constrained devices
TLS variant for UDP-based IoT communication
Remote wireless firmware/software updates for IoT devices
Hardware-based security chip for cryptographic operations
Attack intercepting communication between two parties
Attack overwhelming systems with traffic from multiple sources
Encrypted tunnel for secure remote IoT device access
Interfaces that IoT devices use to communicate with servers

visualTips.visualization: Draw a stack diagram: Bottom = Sensors/Actuators (Perception), Middle = Network (WiFi/Zigbee), Top = Cloud/Analytics (Processing), Very Top = User Apps. Each layer is a 'floor' in a building.
Imagine three lockers: 1) Storage locker (at rest), 2) Delivery truck (in transit), 3) Processing room (in processing). Each needs different locks.
Draw an IoT device as a house: Front door (network), Back door (APIs), Windows (sensors), Foundation (firmware). Each entry point is a potential vulnerability.
Imagine every room in a building has a guard checkpoint - even if you're inside, you still need permission to enter each room. No trusted zones.

analogies.analogy: Like a small backpack vs a warehouse - IoT devices have limited 'space' (memory/processing) compared to computers, so security must be lightweight.
Like a bouncer checking ID at a club door - only allow 'signed' (authorized) software to enter and run, reject anything fake.
Like a army of zombie drones following one command - compromised IoT devices 'wake up' to attack targets when commanded by attacker.
Like compartments in a submarine - if one floods, others stay sealed. Isolating IoT devices limits damage spread.
Like receiving security patches for your phone - but imagine doing it for billions of devices remotely, securely.
